metadata.source: Evidence-based physical therapy for post-stroke gait rehabilitation
references: Journal of NeuroEngineering and Rehabilitation - Gait Training Methods
American Physical Therapy Association - Stroke Rehabilitation Guidelines
Clinical Rehabilitation - Post-Stroke Gait Interventions Meta-Analysis

sensor_requirements.note: All exercises designed for bilateral leg sensor setup

slow_cadence.problem_description: Walking pace below normal range (cadence < 95 steps/min)
slow_cadence.target_improvement: Increase cadence by 10-15 steps/min in 4-6 weeks

severe.name: Metronome-Paced Walking

clinical_evidence.studies: Thaut et al. (2007) - Rhythmic Auditory Stimulation

severe.description: Walk to the beat of a metronome to increase step frequency using auditory cues
instructions: Download a free metronome app on your phone
Set metronome to 60 BPM (beats per minute) to start
Take one step with each beat - left foot on one beat, right foot on next
Walk for 5 minutes continuously
Rest 2 minutes between sets
Complete 3 sets total
Increase by 5 BPM each week (Week 2: 65 BPM, Week 3: 70 BPM)
Goal: Reach 100-110 BPM by week 8
precautions: Use walker or cane if you have balance issues
Stop if you feel dizzy or short of breath
Start with slow tempo and gradually increase
Practice in safe area with support available
Wear non-slip shoes

cadence.method: Count steps per minute from FSR heel strikes

symmetry.method: Compare left vs right step timing

consistency.calculation: Std dev of FSR peak intervals

real_time_feedback: Steps not matching beat - listen carefully
Left leg lagging - focus on symmetry

severe.name: High Knee Marching

clinical_evidence.studies: Pohl et al. (2002) - Task-specific training for stroke
clinical_evidence.success_rate: 65% show improved cadence and knee flexion

severe.description: March in place lifting knees to hip level to strengthen hip flexors and increase step frequency
equipment: Sturdy chair or counter for support
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart
Hold onto chair or counter with one hand for balance
Lift right knee up to hip height (90-degree angle)
Lower right foot and immediately lift left knee
Continue alternating knees in a marching motion
Gradually increase speed while maintaining height
Do 20 marches per leg, rest 1 minute, repeat 3 times
precautions: Keep back straight - don't lean forward
If knee pain occurs, reduce height of knee lift
Stop if you lose balance or feel unsteady
Use support until you feel confident

progression.week_1: 20 reps × 3 sets with support, slow pace
progression.week_2: 25 reps × 3 sets with light support, moderate pace
progression.week_3: 30 reps × 3 sets without support, moderate pace
progression.week_4: 30 reps × 4 sets, add arm swings, faster pace

knee_lift_angle.method: Calculate knee flexion from IMU gyroscope
knee_lift_angle.calculation: Integrate angular velocity during lift phase

repetition_count.method: Count knee flexion peaks per leg
repetition_count.calculation: FSR heel strikes + knee angle peaks

lift_height_consistency.method: Standard deviation of peak knee angles
lift_height_consistency.calculation: Std dev of all knee flexion peaks

symmetry.method: Compare left vs right knee lift angles

real_time_feedback: Right knee not lifting as high as left
Slow down - maintain quality over speed

severe.name: Fast Stepping Drills

clinical_evidence.studies: Lamontagne et al. (2000) - Gait velocity training
clinical_evidence.success_rate: 70% show improvements in maximum walking speed

severe.description: Quick stepping exercises in place or forward to improve step frequency and reaction time
instructions: Stand with feet together near a wall or railing
Take 10 quick small steps forward as fast as you safely can
Focus on speed, not distance
Steps should be rapid and continuous
Rest 30-45 seconds between sets
Repeat 4 times
precautions: Ensure clear path ahead (at least 3 meters)
Practice near wall or railing for safety
Stop if you feel unstable or dizzy
Do not sacrifice form for speed

steps_per_set.method: Count FSR heel strikes in 10-second interval

moderate.name: Interval Walking

clinical_evidence.studies: Macko et al. (2005) - Interval training for stroke rehabilitation
clinical_evidence.success_rate: 82% improvement in walking endurance and speed

moderate.description: Alternate between normal and fast walking speeds to build endurance and increase maximum cadence
instructions: Warm up with 2 minutes of normal walking
Walk at comfortable pace for 2 minutes
Walk as fast as you safely can for 1 minute
Return to comfortable pace for 2 minutes
Repeat fast-normal intervals 5 times
Cool down with 2 minutes slow walking
precautions: Stay within comfortable exertion level
Use assistive device if normally required
Stop if experiencing chest pain or severe breathlessness

interval_detection.method: Identify fast vs normal pace periods
interval_detection.calculation: Compare cadence in fast vs normal windows

real_time_feedback: Recovery interval - return to normal pace

asymmetric_gait.problem_description: Uneven gait pattern with imbalanced weight distribution or unequal step timing
asymmetric_gait.target_improvement: Improve symmetry index from < 0.80 to > 0.90 in 6-8 weeks

severe.name: Single-Leg Stance Training

clinical_evidence.studies: Mercer et al. (2009) - Balance training for stroke

severe.description: Stand on affected leg to improve strength and balance on weaker side
equipment: Sturdy chair or counter for support
instructions: Stand next to sturdy chair or counter
Hold on lightly with one hand
Lift affected (weaker) leg off ground about 6 inches
Balance on standing leg for 30 seconds
Lower leg, rest 15 seconds
Repeat with other leg for comparison
Complete 3 sets per leg
precautions: Keep support within reach at all times
Do not hold breath - breathe normally
Stop if severe pain or instability occurs
benefits: Enhances weight bearing on affected side

weight_bearing.method: FSR total pressure on standing leg
weight_bearing.success_criteria: ≥ 90% of body weight on one foot

stance_duration.method: Time with full weight on one leg

real_time_feedback: Swaying detected - use support if needed

severe.name: Weight-Shifting Drills

clinical_evidence.studies: Cheng et al. (2001) - Weight shifting for stroke rehabilitation

severe.description: Shift weight side-to-side to improve control and awareness of weight distribution
equipment: Parallel bars or two chairs for support
instructions: Stand between parallel bars or holding two chairs
Start with weight evenly distributed
Slowly shift all weight to right leg
Lift left foot slightly off ground (1 inch)
Hold for 3 seconds
Shift weight to left leg
Lift right foot slightly off ground
Hold for 3 seconds
Repeat 15 times per side
precautions: Keep shifts slow and controlled
Maintain upright posture
Use support as needed

weight_transfer_completeness.method: FSR pressure comparison left vs right
weight_transfer_completeness.calculation: Target leg FSR / (left FSR + right FSR)

shift_count.calculation: Number of FSR primary weight changes

hold_duration.method: Time at peak weight on each leg

symmetry_score.method: Balance of left vs right weight bearing time
symmetry_score.calculation: Left total time / right total time

real_time_feedback: Shift more weight to right leg

severe.name: Mirror Walking Practice

clinical_evidence.studies: Altschuler et al. (1999) - Mirror therapy for rehabilitation

severe.description: Walk while watching yourself in mirror to improve visual feedback on gait symmetry
instructions: Position yourself to walk parallel to full-length mirror
Start 2 meters from mirror
Walk slowly forward watching your reflection
Focus on equal step lengths and timing
Observe knee height, arm swing, posture
Walk 10 steps, turn, walk back 10 steps
Rest 1 minute between sets
Repeat 4 times
precautions: Use assistive device if normally required
Focus on mirror briefly to avoid dizziness
Have support nearby

step_length_symmetry.method: Compare stride distances left vs right
step_length_symmetry.calculation: Integration of ankle velocity between steps

step_time_symmetry.method: Compare left vs right stance times
step_time_symmetry.calculation: Left step duration / right step duration

real_time_feedback: Right leg spending more time on ground

moderate.name: Step-Up Exercises (Affected Side)

clinical_evidence.studies: Eng et al. (2002) - Task-specific training for stroke

moderate.description: Step up onto low platform with affected leg to build strength and confidence
moderate.expected_improvement: Improved push-off strength, better step symmetry
instructions: Stand facing low step with railing nearby
Place affected (weaker) foot on step
Push through affected leg to step up
Bring other leg up to meet it
Step back down with other leg first
Affected leg remains on step initially
Lower affected leg back to ground
Repeat 10 times, rest 1 minute, do 3 sets
precautions: Keep railing within reach
Start with low step (4 inches)
Do not twist while stepping
Stop if knee pain occurs
benefits: Strengthens quadriceps and glutes on affected side

repetition_count.method: Detect knee extension peaks on affected leg
repetition_count.calculation: Count knee angle peaks during step-up

knee_extension_angle.calculation: Peak knee angle during standing phase

weight_transfer.method: FSR shows full weight on affected leg during step-up
weight_transfer.calculation: Peak FSR pressure on affected side

real_time_feedback: Push through affected leg - don't pull with arms

short_stride.problem_description: Stride length shorter than normal range (< 1.2m typical)
short_stride.target_improvement: Increase stride length by 10-15cm in 6 weeks

severe.name: Lunge Walking

clinical_evidence.studies: Dean et al. (2000) - Task training for gait after stroke

severe.description: Walking lunges to encourage longer steps and hip extension
equipment: Parallel bars or wall support optional
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart
Take a long step forward with right leg
Lower hips until right knee at 90 degrees
Front knee should be over ankle, not toes
Push through right heel to bring left leg forward
Repeat with left leg taking long step
Continue 10 lunges per leg (20 total)
Rest 1-2 minutes between sets
precautions: Do not let knee extend past toes
Keep torso upright
Use support if balance is challenging
Start with smaller lunges and progress

progression.week_1_2: 10 lunges × 3 sets, short depth, support available
progression.week_3_4: 12 lunges × 3 sets, medium depth, light support
progression.week_5_6: 15 lunges × 3 sets, deeper lunges, no support

benefits: Increases hip flexor and extensor flexibility

control_tempo.method: Time in descent and ascent phases
control_tempo.calculation: Time from peak to valley to peak

left_right_balance.method: Compare lunge counts and depths both legs

real_time_feedback: Slow the descent for better control

severe.name: Visual Target Stepping

clinical_evidence.studies: Hollands et al. (2013) - Visual cues for stride length
clinical_evidence.success_rate: 79% immediate stride length increase with cues

severe.description: Step over visual markers placed at gradually increasing distances to extend stride
equipment: Tape or cones to mark distances
severe.reps_per_set: Walk length of marked path (20 steps)
instructions: Place tape marks on floor at comfortable stride length
Walk forward, placing each foot at a marker
Focus on stepping to each target
After 1 week, increase distance between marks by 5cm
Continue gradual increases weekly
Walk 20 steps per set, rest 1 minute
precautions: Do not over-stride and lose balance
Increase distances gradually
Use assistive device if needed

stride_length_estimate.method: Integrate ankle velocity between heel strikes
stride_length_estimate.calculation: Distance per step from velocity integration

stride_consistency.method: Standard deviation of estimated stride lengths

moderate.name: Heel-to-Toe Walking

clinical_evidence.studies: Tyson et al. (2006) - Gait training interventions

moderate.description: Exaggerated heel-to-toe walking to increase hip extension and stride
instructions: Stand tall with good posture
Step forward landing on heel first
Roll through foot to push off with toes
Exaggerate the heel strike and toe push-off
Extend back leg fully during push-off
Walk slowly and deliberately
Complete 20-meter walk, rest, repeat
precautions: Focus on form over speed
Avoid over-striding which may cause imbalance

heel_strike_detection.success_criteria: Heel FSR peaks before mid/toe FSR
heel_strike_detection.calculation: Timing of FSR heel peak vs mid/toe peaks

toe_push_off.method: FSR toe sensor shows active push-off
toe_push_off.success_criteria: Toe FSR peak at end of stance
toe_push_off.calculation: Toe FSR pressure during terminal stance

roll_through_pattern.calculation: Time delays between FSR sensor peaks

ankle_range.success_criteria: Increased ankle motion during gait cycle
ankle_range.calculation: Min to max ankle angle per step

slow_velocity.target_improvement: Increase velocity by 0.15-0.25 m/s in 6-8 weeks

moderate.name: Overground Speed Walking

clinical_evidence.studies: Sullivan et al. (2007) - Speed-focused gait training

moderate.description: Walk measured distances as fast as safely possible to increase natural walking speed
moderate.reps_per_set: Walk 10 meters as fast as safe
instructions: Mark 10-meter walking path with tape or cones
Stand at start line
When ready, walk as fast as you safely can to end
Time yourself or have someone time you
Rest 2 minutes between trials
Repeat 5 times, trying to improve time each walk
Track your times to monitor progress
precautions: Clear path of obstacles
Do not sacrifice stability for speed
Use assistive device if normally required

velocity_estimate.method: Estimate speed from cadence and stride length

poor_stability.problem_description: Low stability score indicating balance problems during walking
poor_stability.target_improvement: Increase stability score from < 0.65 to > 0.75 in 6-8 weeks

severe.name: Tandem Walking

clinical_evidence.studies: Shumway-Cook et al. (2000) - Balance training for fall prevention

severe.description: Walk heel-to-toe in straight line to improve balance and stability
instructions: Stand at start of straight line with wall nearby
Place right heel directly in front of left toes
Place left heel directly in front of right toes
Continue walking heel-to-toe for 10 steps
Keep arms out to sides for balance
Look straight ahead, not down
Rest 1 minute, repeat 3 times
precautions: Keep support within arm's reach
Do not attempt if recently dizzy
Start with fingertips on wall if needed

lateral_sway.method: Ankle IMU accelerometer variance in lateral axis

real_time_feedback: Balance correction detected - slow down if needed

severe.name: Multisurface Balance Training

clinical_evidence.studies: Mirelman et al. (2016) - Balance training on varied surfaces

severe.description: Walk on different surfaces (carpet, tile, grass) to challenge balance systems
instructions: Start on firm flat surface (tile, concrete)
Walk 20 steps normally
Move to softer surface (carpet, mat)
Walk 20 steps, noting balance challenges
If safe, try outdoor grass or uneven surface
Walk 20 steps carefully
Repeat sequence 3 times
precautions: Have someone nearby for outdoor surfaces
Avoid truly hazardous terrain
Use assistive device on challenging surfaces

progression.week_5_6: Add outdoor grass if available and safe

surface_adaptation.success_criteria: Improved stability on challenging surfaces over time
surface_adaptation.calculation: Week-over-week reduction in IMU variance on soft surfaces

steps_on_each_surface.method: Count steps during each surface walking bout
steps_on_each_surface.calculation: FSR step counts per walking interval

sway_comparison.method: Ankle sway on firm vs soft surfaces
sway_comparison.calculation: SD of IMU on soft surface / SD on firm surface

real_time_feedback: Week 4: 30% less sway on carpet vs week 1

moderate.name: Core Strengthening for Gait

clinical_evidence.studies: Verheyden et al. (2006) - Trunk training for stroke balance
clinical_evidence.success_rate: 65% show improved trunk control and stability

moderate.description: Standing exercises to strengthen core muscles that support stable walking
instructions: Stand behind chair holding backrest
Stand on both feet, weight evenly distributed
Slowly march in place, lifting knees moderately
Keep torso stable and upright (no swaying)
Do 15 marches per leg
Rest 1 minute
Repeat 3 sets
precautions: Maintain upright posture
Don't lean on chair - use for balance only

trunk_stability.method: If waist IMU present, measure trunk sway

reduced_step_regularity.problem_description: Inconsistent step timing and rhythm during walking
reduced_step_regularity.target_improvement: Improve step regularity score from < 0.75 to > 0.85 in 4-6 weeks

severe.name: Rhythmic Auditory Cueing

clinical_evidence.studies: Thaut et al. (1997) - RAS for gait rehabilitation

severe.description: Walk to consistent beat of music or metronome to establish regular rhythm
equipment: Music player with steady beat or metronome
instructions: Select music with clear, steady beat (90-100 BPM)
Walk taking one step per beat
Focus on matching rhythm perfectly
Walk continuously for 5 minutes
Rest 2 minutes
Repeat 3 times per session
precautions: Choose music without tempo changes
Keep volume at safe level if using headphones
Practice in safe environment

step_timing_variability.method: Calculate coefficient of variation of inter-step intervals
step_timing_variability.calculation: (SD / mean) × 100 of FSR heel strike intervals

rhythm_matching.method: Compare step timing to target BPM

real_time_feedback: Slight rushing - slow down to match beat

moderate.name: Paced Walking Practice

clinical_evidence.studies: Rochester et al. (2010) - Cueing for gait regularity

moderate.description: Walk at self-paced consistent rhythm, focusing on feeling the regularity
instructions: Start walking at comfortable pace
Focus on making each step the same as the last
Count 1-2-3-4 silently to establish rhythm
Walk 50 steps maintaining the rhythm
Rest 1 minute
Repeat 3 times
precautions: Don't rush - prioritize consistency over speed

progression.week_3_4: 75 steps × 3 sets, reduced attention to feet
